questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spinning wheel is brought into contact with an identical wheel spinning at an identical speed. The wheels slow down under the influence of friction. Which of the following energies of the first wheel decrease?
questions.solution:
As the wheel slows down due to friction, its angular velocity decreases. Therefore, its kinetic energy decreases.

Mechanical energy is the sum of kinetic and potential energy. Since the potential energy remains constant and the kinetic energy decreases, the mechanical energy also decreases.

The work done by friction generates heat, which raises the temperature of the wheels. This causes an increase in the internal energy of the wheels.

By symmetry, the heat generated is shared equally between the two identical wheels. The decrease in the mechanical energy of the first wheel is exactly compensated by the increase in its internal energy. Thus, the total energy of the first wheel remains constant.

Hence, only the mechanical and kinetic energies of the first wheel decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spinning wheel $A$ is brought into contact with another wheel $B$ that is initially at rest. Because of friction at the contact point, the second wheel also begins to spin. Which of the following energies of wheel $B$ increase?
questions.solution:
When wheel $A$ is brought into contact with wheel $B$, kinetic friction acts on wheel $B$ at the point of contact.

This frictional force provides a torque that causes wheel $B$ to start rotating. As wheel $B$ accelerates from rest, its rotational kinetic energy increases.

Mechanical energy is the sum of kinetic energy and potential energy. Since the kinetic energy of wheel $B$ increases while its potential energy remains constant (assuming its center of mass does not change height), the total mechanical energy of wheel $B$ increases.

Furthermore, the relative slipping between the two wheels at the contact point generates heat due to friction. This heat raises the temperature of wheel $B$, which means its internal energy also increases.

Therefore, the internal energy, mechanical energy, and kinetic energy of wheel $B$ all increase.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body $A$ rests on a railway platform while an identical body $B$ is inside a moving train. From the perspective of the ground, which of the following energies of $B$ exceed those of $A$?
questions.solution:
Body $A$ is at rest on the ground, so its macroscopic velocity $v_A = 0$.

Body $B$ is in a moving train, so its macroscopic velocity $v_B > 0$ with respect to the ground.

Kinetic energy of $A$ is zero, while kinetic energy of $B$ is $\dfrac{1}{2}mv_B^2 > 0$. Thus, the kinetic energy of $B$ exceeds that of $A$.

Mechanical energy is the sum of kinetic and potential energy. Assuming both bodies are at the same reference level, their potential energies are equal. Hence, the mechanical energy of $B$ is greater than that of $A$.

Internal energy depends only on the internal state (temperature, molecular arrangement) of the body and is independent of the macroscopic frame of reference. Since $A$ and $B$ are identical, their internal energies are equal.

Total energy is the sum of mechanical energy and internal energy. Since the mechanical energy of $B$ is greater and internal energies are equal, the total energy of $B$ exceeds that of $A$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As the temperature rises, the time period of a pendulum
questions.solution:
The time period of a simple pendulum is given by $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$

As the temperature increases, the length $l$ of the pendulum increases due to linear thermal expansion, given by $l' = l(1 + \alpha \Delta \theta)$

Since $T \propto \sqrt{l}$, an increase in the length of the pendulum leads to an increase in its time period.

Thus, the time period of the pendulum increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For which of the following pairs of temperature scales is the size of a degree identical?
options: Celsius scale and ideal gas scale
Ideal gas scale and absolute scale
Mercury scale and ideal gas scale
questions.solution:
The size of a degree on the Celsius scale is defined to be exactly equal to the size of a degree on the absolute (Kelvin) scale. A temperature difference of $1^{\circ}\text{C}$ is equivalent to a temperature difference of $1\text{ K}$.

The ideal gas temperature scale is based on the properties of an ideal gas and is theoretically identical to the absolute thermodynamic temperature scale (Kelvin scale). Therefore, the size of a degree on the ideal gas scale is identical to that on the absolute scale.

Since the Celsius, ideal gas, and absolute scales all share the same degree size, the pairs (Celsius scale and ideal gas scale) and (ideal gas scale and absolute scale) have identical degree sizes.

The mercury scale is an empirical scale based on the volumetric expansion of mercury. Because the coefficient of expansion of mercury is not perfectly constant with temperature, the size of a degree on the mercury scale is not strictly identical to the thermodynamic or ideal gas scales at all temperatures.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For some time, a solid object is kept in water inside an adiabatic container. The temperature of the water decreases during this period, and there is no appreciable change in the shape of the object. The temperature of the solid object
questions.solution:
Since the container is adiabatic, no heat is exchanged with the surroundings. The decrease in the temperature of the water indicates that the water loses heat.

This heat must be absorbed by the solid object. The absorbed heat can either increase the temperature of the solid object or be utilized in a phase change.

If the heat increases the thermal energy of the solid, its temperature will increase.

If the solid is at its melting point, it will absorb heat to undergo a phase change, during which its temperature remains constant. The condition that there is no appreciable change in shape allows for a very small amount of melting that does not visibly alter the shape, or a solid-solid phase transition.

Therefore, the temperature of the solid object may have increased or may have remained constant.
